sections.paper_id: Nah et al. - 2025 - Revisiting citizen journalism scholarship in the Web Era (1994-2023) Past, present, and prospect
paragraphs: The study revisits citizen journalism scholarship spanning 30 years of journal articles published in the fields of journalism, communication, media, and technology studies. Previous studies in this domain have attempted to assess the state of citizen journalism research since the introduction of the Internet and its Web technologies in 1994 (e.g., Moon et al., 2020;Nah, 2008;Wall, 2017). However, it remains fully unexplored concerning the topology and knowledge network structure of the scholarship on citizen journalism in a more holistic and systematic way. This question is of particular significance given the recent decline in citizen journalism scholarship. Thus, this study provides an exploratory review of the field of citizen journalism in the context of journalism and democracy and further discusses the past, present, and prospects for future directions. It aims to advance citizen journalism scholarship with future directions in terms of theory, research, practice, and policy implications, nationally, and globally. Thus, the study posits the following research questions. Specifically, RQ1: what is the topology of the main research subjects and trajectories within contemporary citizen journalism scholarship?
RQ2: what is the knowledge structure encompassing the co-authorship network in the sphere of citizen journalism?

While the study contributes to the existing body of knowledge on citizen journalism scholarship, it also stimulates ongoing intellectual inquiry within an international and interdisciplinary context. In pursuit of these goals, the study evaluates citizen journalism scholarship over the last three decades employing a diverse and blended array of research methodologies.

Our selection criteria for citizen journalism literature are twofold. First, we adopted a conceptual definition of citizen journalism, which refers to contributions to the democratic conversation in the public sphere (Friedland and Kim, 2009;Nah, 2008) and encompasses participatory journalism as part of the broader concept of citizen journalism in its historical evolution (Nip, 2006). Based on this definition, our search criteria included a wide range of citizen journalists and their activities: (a) UGC (user-generated content): Posting comments on news stories, responding to comments, and uploading photos/videos to news sites; (b) Participatory journalism: posting news stories to news sites and collaborating with professional journalists on news stories; (c) Citizen journalism: Writing news stories independently, such as in mainstream media, legacy media, citizen news media, social media, and blogs. In other words, we included journal articles that discussed these various forms of citizen journalism activities in the contexts of journalism, newsrooms, journalists, professionalism, and journalistic ethics.

Second, we relied on Bourdieu's field theory (e.g., Benson and Neveu, 2005) and the diversity of the public sphere in which professional and citizen journalism both compete and collaborate, complementing each other within journalistic, political, economic, and civic domains. As both professional and citizen journalism are entrenched not only within journalistic fields but also within society at large, we incorporated studies that examined the adoption and utilization of citizen journalism by professionals. We also included studies that conducted comparative analyses of content, public perception, trust, and evaluations regarding professional journalism versus citizen journalism.

In the following sections, we first provide a more detailed explanation of our search strategy and mixed-method analysis approach. Second, we present the results from topic modeling and semantic network analysis, bibliometric analysis, and manual coding. Lastly, we discuss the limitations and future directions of citizen journalism scholarship.

sections.paper_id: Nah et al. - 2025 - Revisiting citizen journalism scholarship in the Web Era (1994-2023) Past, present, and prospect
Nah et al. - 2025 - Revisiting citizen journalism scholarship in the Web Era (1994-2023) Past, present, and prospect
paragraphs: In conducting a literature review that encompasses significant and relevant studies, we initiated our search with widely recognized databases such as the Social Sciences Citation Index (SSCI) and Science Citation Index Expanded (SCI-EXPANDED) on the Web of Science (WOS). In doing so, we used a comprehensive list of keywords to search articles related to citizen journalism (see below). Specifically, we identified articles that (1) contain the keywords in the abstract; (2) are published in 1994-2023; (3) are academic journal articles; (4) are in English and included them for final manual filtering. This search yielded a total of 1,772 articles: 395 articles are in the communication category and 1,377 in non-communication related fields, respectively.

("citizen journalism" or "participatory journalism" or "citizen journalists" or "participatory journalists" or "citizen reporters" or "participatory reporters" or "citizen contributors" or "participatory contributors" or "cyberjournalists" or "usergenerated content" or "user-created content") One of the researchers first coded all 395 articles in the communication category and grouped them into three categories by reading the abstracts: (1) included, (2) need further investigation, and (3) not included. Articles containing "user-generated content" or "user-created content" in particular were only included when they focus on the journalistic functions of user-generated content. We then paired four trained coders to validate the coding. For articles that are included, two coders verified 20% random samples by reading the title, abstract, and journal name. For those that need further investigation, two coders evaluated the articles by reading the title, abstract, journal name, and full text. Lastly, another pair of coders coded all articles that were not included by reading the title, abstract, and journal name. Our intercoder reliability reaches a 96% agreement rate on average. The four coders then coded the remaining 1,377 articles independently (Krippendorff, 2018).

To complement the search from the WOS database, we further searched for articles on JSTOR and EBSCO. We adopted similar search strategies, which yielded 1,976 results on JSTOR and 632 results on EBSCO, respectively. Four coders first checked every 20th article in the search results and decided whether these articles should be included for further analysis or not. We only included those articles that have not been found in the WOS database.

After this iterative filtering process, we identified in total 275 articles for further analysis, which were published not only in communication and journalism but also in other disciplines such as sociology and information and data science. Figure 1 below shows the year trend of articles included in our analysis, displaying a drastically decreasing trend since 2017.

sections.paper_id: Nah et al. - 2025 - Revisiting citizen journalism scholarship in the Web Era (1994-2023) Past, present, and prospect
paragraphs: The study employs three distinct but complementary methods to analyze the data collected on citizen journalism scholarship: topic modeling, bibliometric analysis, and manual content analysis.

First, we use topic modeling and semantic network analysis to identify and visualize the major topics in journal articles related to citizen journalism scholarship (Walter and Ophir, 2019). We run the analysis on article abstract and introduction sections because the two parts sufficiently convey the main research question(s), background, methodology, and results of an article. Specifically, we first preprocessed our corpora including tokenization and removing numbers, special characters, and English stopwords. Second, we ran a 10-fold cross-validation to identify the optimal number of k (the number of topics in each document) and alpha (document topic density) on the corpora. Then, we applied the Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) algorithm to identify topics in our corpora with the optimal alpha and k values, which are 0.1 and 40, respectively. We then manually labeled each topic based on the top keywords, top FREX words (frequent and exclusive), and most representative documents of the topic. Lastly, we clustered and visualized the topics using the Walktrap algorithm on Gephi (Bastian et al., 2009).

Second, we run bibliometric analysis using VosViewer to analyze the knowledge network structure of citizen journalism scholarship. VosViewer is a widely used software tool for constructing and visualizing bibliometric networks ( Van Eck and Waltman, 2010). Specifically, we ran co-authorship analysis in terms of authors, organizations, and countries. We also visualized journal citation and literature cocitation relationships to investigate the common reference related to citizen journalism. Additionally, we also analyzed the keyword co-occurrence to examine the major topics.

Third, we conduct manual content analysis to delve deeper into the theory, methods, practices, ethical and legal considerations, and implications for policy in citizen journalism scholarship, as well as professionalism, interaction between professional and citizen journalism, volunteerism, community building, and civic engagement (Nah and Chung, 2020). We developed a coding scheme to capture the information that complements the results from topic modeling and bibliometric analysis. The coding scheme measures the basic information of a research article (including theory, method, country of interest, and the unit of analysis), specifics of citizen journalism, such as the platforms, types of relationships between professional journalists and traditional journalism, and the type of medium, and the outcomes related to civic engagement and political participation(See Appendix C). We first randomly sampled 10 articles for coder training. Second, four coders coded 20% of a random sample in pairs. We reached acceptable intercoder reliability for all variables (Krippendorf's alpha: 0.901 for coder pair one and 0.814 for coder pair 2). The four coders then coded the rest of the articles independently.

Nah et al. - 2025 - Revisiting citizen journalism scholarship in the Web Era (1994-2023) Past, present, and prospect
sections.header_raw: Topic modeling and semantic network analysis
sections.header_norm: topic modeling and semantic network analysis
paragraphs: The first topic cluster in purple on the top of Figure 2 deals with the digital technology impact on citizen journalism. Specifically, it examines the impact of digitally networked technologies, the Internet, and blogs on citizen news production, political process, and civic engagement (J öuet, 2009;Hung, 2013). For instance, Moyo (2009) examines how SMS and web blogs circulate public opinion, providing alternative discourse in addition to mainstream media under a repressive regime. Nah and Yamamoto (2019) used the survey method to examine the interrelationship between expressive use of the Internet and mobile devices, integrated connectedness to a storytelling network, and civic participation. They find that the positive link between the connection to a storytelling network and civic participation is stronger for those who have a higher level of expressive use of the Internet and mobile devices on local affairs. In addition to facilitating audience participation, web blogs are also found to influence intermedia agenda setting (Meraz, 2011), which has the potential to impact campaign communication (Lawson-Borders and Kirk, 2005).

Additionally, another body of work under this topic cluster addresses specific citizen journalism empowerment projects (Ambala, 2016), the vitality of citizen journalism in resource-poor areas with a particular focus on India (Chadha and Steiner, 2015), and how those citizen journalists and citizen journalism projects/initiatives evolve and determinant factors (Luce et al., 2017).

While this body of research implies a positive link between citizen journalism and the political process, another important work by Ceron (2015) finds an opposite relationship. Specifically, it finds that the information consumption on social media is linked with lower political trust, while getting information from news websites is positively related to political trust. The author argues that the difference in news quality between professional journalism and citizen journalism is an important determinant of trust. Following this idea, Straub-Cook (2018) analyzes user-generated content related to local public affairs on Seattle's subreddit, and finds that citizengenerated content does not adhere to professional journalism standards, even though citizens draw from a variety of information sources to contest legacy media in their conversations.

The topic cluster in red focuses on approaches to audience engagement by professional journalists (PJ) and its impact on professional journalists' role in news production (Hänska-Ahy and Shapour, 2013;Wardle and Williams, 2010). For instance, Lawrence et al. (2018) find that media outlets are already experimenting with tools for audience engagement, yet this engagement is limited to back-end reaction to contents generated by professional journalists, rather than involving the audience in content creation. McElroy (2019) finds more complex results by interviewing newsroom employees in the United States about newsroom culture and the impact of audience interaction. Although news consumers may not be directly involved in news production, professional journalists may use UGC by following their online conversations, which potentially impacts the news gathering process. The author concludes that the traditional role of professional journalists in gatekeeping has been shifted but not entirely replaced (p. 462).

This finding is echoed in another work by Mabweazara (2016), which examines reader comments on leading news websites in Zimbabwe. The study finds that the comment forums serve as a space for public deliberation, which complement traditional journalistic practices in providing story ideas. However, the study also notes the risk of abusive and extremist comments with the lack of effective gatekeeping strategies (p. 58). On the other hand, another study on BBC's UGC hub by Harrison (2010) argues that this challenge should not be overstated as UGC is carefully moderated to ensure it does not challenge BBC's editorial values (p. 252-255).

Under this context, studies have started to rethink the boundaries of "journalist" and "news media", its legislative and policy implications (Johnston and Wallace, 2017), and the nature and characteristics of participatory journalism (Borger et al., 2016;Scott et al., 2015). A body of work examines how professional journalists perceive and navigate their identity in the context of social media, user-generated content, and other online communication technologies (Ferrucci and Vos, 2017;Tong, 2015). Additionally, another stream of research seeks to understand the role of citizen journalism in the business and reporting models of media organizations (Ibahrine, 2020;Nixon, 2020).

The last topic cluster in green focuses on the new modality of citizen journalism and civic participation. The backdrop of this line of research is that social media and other digital technologies have given ordinary people the capacity to report emergencies, providing news ideas and alternative perspectives for professional journalists (Norris, 2017), which may shape public conversation and civic participation. Discussions on different modalities of citizen journalism enabled by new technologies, such as citizen photojournalism and video journalism are also included in this line of research (Borges-Rey, 2015;Yeo, 2016).

Specifically, studies examine how citizen journalists shape the dominant discourse and narrative of traditional media in cases such as crisis, violence, and conflicts (e.g., bombing, police shooting, disasters, epidemics, and humanitarian crises (Chernobrov, 2018;Pearce and Rodgers, 2020). For instance, Spyksma (2019) examines the role of non-governmental organizations in "eyewitness reporting" using the case of Cyclone Pam in Vanuatu in 2015. The author suggests that NGO's coverage fills the news gap about the storm by providing perspectives from the Pacific region (p. 8-14). Similarly, Walker (2022) argues that citizen journalism, social media, and other digital technologies may impact journalistic reporting about police shootings due to their advantage in "speed and accessibility, accuracy, and a multi-layered challenge to police authority" (p. 2022). In addition to providing alternative information, studies have also examined the role of real-time citizen reporting in activism and mobilization with a particular focus on Twitter (Veenstra et al., 2014a;2014b).

While some studies find that citizen journalism on Twitter has the capacity to influence public conversation and civic engagement, Kermani and Adham (2021) challenge the idea of digital democracy. Their study examining the Twittersphere during Iran's presidential election of 2017 suggests that mainstream media and journalists still remain as a powerful information source despite that other users with diversified political interests (particularly the marginalized group) have different voices (p. 12-13).
sections.text:
The first topic cluster in purple on the top of Figure 2 deals with the digital technology impact on citizen journalism. Specifically, it examines the impact of digitally networked technologies, the Internet, and blogs on citizen news production, political process, and civic engagement (J öuet, 2009;Hung, 2013). For instance, Moyo (2009) examines how SMS and web blogs circulate public opinion, providing alternative discourse in addition to mainstream media under a repressive regime. Nah and Yamamoto (2019) used the survey method to examine the interrelationship between expressive use of the Internet and mobile devices, integrated connectedness to a storytelling network, and civic participation. They find that the positive link between the connection to a storytelling network and civic participation is stronger for those who have a higher level of expressive use of the Internet and mobile devices on local affairs. In addition to facilitating audience participation, web blogs are also found to influence intermedia agenda setting (Meraz, 2011), which has the potential to impact campaign communication (Lawson-Borders and Kirk, 2005).

Additionally, another body of work under this topic cluster addresses specific citizen journalism empowerment projects (Ambala, 2016), the vitality of citizen journalism in resource-poor areas with a particular focus on India (Chadha and Steiner, 2015), and how those citizen journalists and citizen journalism projects/initiatives evolve and determinant factors (Luce et al., 2017).

While this body of research implies a positive link between citizen journalism and the political process, another important work by Ceron (2015) finds an opposite relationship. Specifically, it finds that the information consumption on social media is linked with lower political trust, while getting information from news websites is positively related to political trust. The author argues that the difference in news quality between professional journalism and citizen journalism is an important determinant of trust. Following this idea, Straub-Cook (2018) analyzes user-generated content related to local public affairs on Seattle's subreddit, and finds that citizengenerated content does not adhere to professional journalism standards, even though citizens draw from a variety of information sources to contest legacy media in their conversations.

The topic cluster in red focuses on approaches to audience engagement by professional journalists (PJ) and its impact on professional journalists' role in news production (Hänska-Ahy and Shapour, 2013;Wardle and Williams, 2010). For instance, Lawrence et al. (2018) find that media outlets are already experimenting with tools for audience engagement, yet this engagement is limited to back-end reaction to contents generated by professional journalists, rather than involving the audience in content creation. McElroy (2019) finds more complex results by interviewing newsroom employees in the United States about newsroom culture and the impact of audience interaction. Although news consumers may not be directly involved in news production, professional journalists may use UGC by following their online conversations, which potentially impacts the news gathering process. The author concludes that the traditional role of professional journalists in gatekeeping has been shifted but not entirely replaced (p. 462).

This finding is echoed in another work by Mabweazara (2016), which examines reader comments on leading news websites in Zimbabwe. The study finds that the comment forums serve as a space for public deliberation, which complement traditional journalistic practices in providing story ideas. However, the study also notes the risk of abusive and extremist comments with the lack of effective gatekeeping strategies (p. 58). On the other hand, another study on BBC's UGC hub by Harrison (2010) argues that this challenge should not be overstated as UGC is carefully moderated to ensure it does not challenge BBC's editorial values (p. 252-255).

Under this context, studies have started to rethink the boundaries of "journalist" and "news media", its legislative and policy implications (Johnston and Wallace, 2017), and the nature and characteristics of participatory journalism (Borger et al., 2016;Scott et al., 2015). A body of work examines how professional journalists perceive and navigate their identity in the context of social media, user-generated content, and other online communication technologies (Ferrucci and Vos, 2017;Tong, 2015). Additionally, another stream of research seeks to understand the role of citizen journalism in the business and reporting models of media organizations (Ibahrine, 2020;Nixon, 2020).

The last topic cluster in green focuses on the new modality of citizen journalism and civic participation. The backdrop of this line of research is that social media and other digital technologies have given ordinary people the capacity to report emergencies, providing news ideas and alternative perspectives for professional journalists (Norris, 2017), which may shape public conversation and civic participation. In addition to providing alternative information, studies have also examined the role of real-time citizen reporting in activism and mobilization with a particular focus on Twitter (Veenstra et al., 2014a;2014b).

Their study examining the Twittersphere during Iran's presidential election of 2017 suggests that mainstream media and journalists still remain as a powerful information source despite that other users with diversified political interests (particularly the marginalized group) have different voices (p. 12-13).

sections.paper_id: Nah et al. - 2025 - Revisiting citizen journalism scholarship in the Web Era (1994-2023) Past, present, and prospect
paragraphs: Author co-authorship network. The layout of the density map implies that citizen journalism scholars frequently engage in collaborative efforts. However, the majority of this type of cluster remains isolated from each other. This indicates that citizen journalism scholars tend to collaborate within small groups and may have limited communication beyond their existing working groups.

Institute and countries/regions co-authorship. Figure 5 displays the co-authorship network among research institutes at the time those articles related to citizen journalism were published. The University of Kentucky ranks first with 15 articles. This is followed by the University of Oregon (12 counts), SUNY Albany (7 counts), the University of Iowa Figure 6 shows the country/region co-authorship network by average publication year. The USA has the highest number of publications among all the countries/regions (121 counts). This is followed by England (31 counts), Australia (14 counts), South Africa (13 counts), and Netherlands (12 counts). In terms of co-authorship, the USA has the largest degree among all the countries/regions (13 counts, i.e., publications with 13 different countries), which is followed by Netherlands (5 counts), Austria (5 counts), Switzerland (4 counts), and Spain (4 counts). The year trend suggests that the majority of publications in countries, such as England, Australia, Netherlands, and Sweden, were published in early periods. On the other hand, the USA's publications are relatively more evenly distributed across all the years. Recent scholarship has started to expand to other locations, including Chile, Iran, Japan, Russia, Spain, and Taiwan. The overall layout of the network also suggests that highly productive countries/regions generally tend to collaborate with each other. Smaller nodes in the peripheral areas (e.g., Turkey, Singapore, and Belgium) are not included in the co-authorship clusters around the major nodes. Journal citation network. Figure 7 illustrates the journal citation network where each node represents a journal and an edge between journals means that a journal has cited the other in the past. The size of each node represents the number of times a journal has been cited. The figure implies that Journalism Practice, Journalism Studies, Journalism, and New media & Society are the most influential journals in the field related to citizen journalism. Specifically, the rank in terms of the total number of citations shows that Journalism Practice (479 counts), Journalism (421 counts), New Media & Society (398 counts), Journalism Studies (368 counts), and Digital journalism (228 counts) are the top five most cited journals.

Literature co-citation network. The results (See Figure 8) highlight that the article, titled, Social news, citizen journalism and democracy by Goode (2009) has the highest links (81 counts), meaning that it is widely recognized and has significant impact in this field of research. This is followed by A clash of cultures: The integration of user-generated content within professional journalistic frameworks at British newspaper websites by Hermida and Thurman (2008), Participatory Journalism Practices in the Media and Beyond: An International Comparative Study of Initiatives in Online Newspapers by Domingo et al. (2008), and We the media: technology empowers a new grassroots journalism by Gillmor (2004) with 79 links. In general, the literature co-citation networks under the audience engagement and PJ identity theme and new modality and civic engagement theme have more clusters than the digital technology impact theme. It implies that there is a significant amount of communication among multiple research areas in those two themes (See Appendix A).

sections.paper_id: Nah et al. - 2025 - Revisiting citizen journalism scholarship in the Web Era (1994-2023) Past, present, and prospect
paragraphs: Figure 9 presents the keyword network based on co-occurrence where each node denotes an individual keyword and the edge between two nodes represents the cooccurrence of the keywords in an article. The size of each node indicates the number of times that a keyword appears in articles. The result that "citizen journalism" (116 times), "news" (69 times), "media" (62 times), "journalism" (49 times), and "user-generated content" (42 times) are frequently used in the literature is intuitive as these terms are the same as or very similar to our search keywords. The popularity of keywords, such as "social media" (50 times), "internet" (35 times), "twitter" (20 times), "online" (17 times), "web" (14 times), and "new media" (15 times), implies that prior literature largely focuses on citizen journalism from the perspective of digital technology and social media. Secondly, the usage of other keywords, including "community" (18 times), "public sphere" (16 times), and "democracy" (15 times), shows that prior literature also emphasizes the impact of citizen journalism on community building and democracy. However, this aspect is relatively less studied compared to digital technology and social media. The year trend indicates that the keywords, such as "internet", "new media", "digital media", "web", "blog", "newspaper", "web 2.0", and "YouTube" (nodes in darker purple color), were used more often in the early periods, implying that earlier scholarship focuses on digital media impact on citizen journalism. recent keywords (nodes in yellow) include "engagement", "civic participation", "neighborhood", "communication infrastructure theory", "Syria", "witnessing", "journalism ethics", and "fake news". It indicates that recent scholarship starts to examine citizen journalism's role in civic outcomes, citizen journalism in other authoritarian regimes, and ethical issues with respect to relying on citizen-generated content as an information source.

We visualize keyword co-occurrence by thematic areas in Figures 10 to 12. Figure 10 shows the keyword co-occurrence under the digital technology impact theme. We observe that "social media", "internet", "new media", "twitter", "web", and "online news" are the important keywords in addition to words referring to citizen journalism (e.g., "citizen journalism", "journalism", "news"). It implies that this body of literature focuses on various aspects of digital technology and citizen journalism, which aligns with our findings from the topic modeling analysis. Additionally, the year trend shows that keywords, such as "terrorism", "web 2.0", "authority", "alternative journalism", "bloggers", "civil society", were more often used in earlier periods. Scholarly work has recently started to examine citizen journalism influence on political discussion and engagement and citizen journalism projects in various countries such as India, Zimbabwe, and other nations in Africa. Figure 11 shows the keyword co-occurrence under the audience engagement and professional journalists' identity theme. In general, the node color indicates that this group of keywords is more often used in earlier periods compared to the keywords under the digital technology impact theme. Keywords, such as "fake news", "verification", and "metrics", are used more frequently in recent years, implying that the citizen journalism scholarship has started to examine the potential risks associated with using user-generated content in news production.

Figure 12 shows the keyword co-occurrence under the modality and civic participation theme. "photojournalism", "images", "photos", "camera", and "video" implies that this body of articles is concerned with those new modalities of citizen journalism using photos and videos to document an important event (e.g., "disaster response", "conflict", "humanitarian crisis", and "wisconsin protests").

Scholarly work has recently started to examine citizen journalism influence on political discussion and engagement and citizen journalism projects in various countries such as India, Zimbabwe, and other nations in Africa. Figure 11 shows the keyword co-occurrence under the audience engagement and professional journalists' identity theme. In general, the node color indicates that this group of keywords is more often used in earlier periods compared to the keywords under the digital technology impact theme. Keywords, such as "fake news", "verification", and "metrics", are used more frequently in recent years, implying that the citizen journalism scholarship has started to examine the potential risks associated with using user-generated content in news production.

Figure 12 shows the keyword co-occurrence under the modality and civic participation theme. "photojournalism", "images", "photos", "camera", and "video" implies that this body of articles is concerned with those new modalities of citizen journalism using photos and videos to document an important event (e.g., "disaster response", "conflict", "humanitarian crisis", and "wisconsin protests").

paragraphs: Basic article information. The year trend of articles by theory indicates that gatekeeping theory is the most frequently used theory (26 counts). Studies applying this theory often focus on the gatekeeping function of legacy media and professional journalists or how this role has been impacted by participatory journalism (Chung and Nah, 2014;Dovbysh, 2021;Saldana et al., Figure 12. Keyword co-occurrence by average publication year under the new modality and civic participation theme.

2017). This is followed by public sphere theory (15 counts), agenda-setting theory (9 counts), and communicative action theory (6 counts). The year trend shows that recent scholarship has turned to focus on gatekeeping and agenda-setting theories since 2019. It is worth noting that nearly half of the articles (107 articles) did not apply a specific theoretical framework in their empirical studies. These articles are often exploratory and descriptive, with no intention to examine the relationship between different factors, such as the impact of a specific citizen journalism platform on an outcome variable.

The year trend of articles indicates that content analysis method ranks first with 68 counts. Studies often use this method to investigate the content of citizen or usercreated content (Wold, 2022;Meijer 2013). This is followed by interview (56 times), theoretical/commentary (44 times), survey (39 times), and observation (16 times). It indicates that prior citizen journalism literature largely relies on qualitative methods. The year trend shows that the number of articles using the theoretical/commentary approaches has been gradually decreasing while the use of other empirical methods has been increasing in recent years.

Lastly, the year trend of articles by the unit of analysis indicates that individual(s) ranks first with 105 counts, encompassing individual citizen journalists, professional editors, professional journalists, and other types of study participants (e.g., students). Platform(s) ranks second with 85 counts, which include both legacy and social media platforms, as well as traditional and citizen journalist news websites. This is followed by citizen journalism in general (12 counts). This result implies that prior literature often approaches citizen journalism from the perspective of individual actors (e.g., survey of journalists' views on user-created content and citizen journalism, Wardle and Williams, 2010;Luce et al., 2017). An important body of research also examines this phenomenon from the perspective of specific platforms (e.g., the organizational structure of citizen journalism websites, Lee, 2012;Chernobrov, 2018). Lastly, the trend indicates that the distribution of shares for the three types of units of analysis has remained stable over the years.

Types of citizen journalism. In order to investigate how prior work approaches citizen journalism and from which perspective, we counted how many times a specific type of citizen journalism platform, medium, and relationship with professional journalists were examined in prior work. In terms of the year trend of citizen journalism scholarship by platform, mainstream or citizen-based news sites are the most studied types of citizen journalism platforms with 100 counts. This category includes traditional media websites where users can comment online and citizen-based news sites and report citizen-created content. This is followed by blogs (40 counts), social media in general (34 counts), and specific social media platforms (e.g., Facebook, 32 counts). The year trend shows that the share of social media has been increasing since 2017, while articles focusing on news sites have been decreasing.

In terms of the year trend of articles by medium type of citizen journalism, text is still the major focus of prior work (102 counts). Video (75 counts) and image (65 counts, e.g., photojournalism) are starting to be more important in recent citizen journalism scholarship (Fernández-Martínez, 2019;Hadland et al., 2016;Palomo and Guerrero-García, 2015;Paschalidis, 2017).

Lastly, the trend by relationship between citizen journalists and professional journalists shows that the majority of work focuses on how citizens produce news on their own or comment on traditional media news sites or social media (123 times). This is followed by traditional media outlets incorporating user-generated content (43 times). The other two types, citizens and journalists jointly create content (29 times) and individual professional journalists adopt user-created content are relatively less studied (8 times). The shares have remained the same across the years, but we observe a decrease in the number of articles focusing on incorporating user-generated content by traditional media outlets after 2017.

Outcomes of citizen journalism on civic engagement and participation. Since citizen journalism has been theorized to have an impact on civic engagement and participation, we examined the trend of specific settings and types of participation in prior work. Online participation is the main focus of prior work (85 times), while offline participation is less studied (31 times). The year trend also indicates that online participation has remained the main focus of prior citizen journalism scholarship over the years.

More specifically, protests are the most studied type of participation, which includes both online and offline activism and mobilization (17 times). Local public affairs and community building ranks second with 16 counts. Facilitating crisis reporting and management (9 times) and increasing political participation (9 times) have also been examined in prior citizen journalism literature. It is worth noting that 109 articles out of the articles we coded do not provide further discussions on participation settings, and 151 articles do not address the specific types of civic engagement (e.g. Chia, 2012;Tsai et al., 2022;Watson, 2011), implying that this aspect is less studied in prior literature (See Appendix B).

sections.paper_id: Nah et al. - 2025 - Revisiting citizen journalism scholarship in the Web Era (1994-2023) Past, present, and prospect
paragraphs: Our meta-analytical study maps out the citizen journalism scholarship spanning the last 30 years of journal articles published in the fields of journalism, communication, media, technology studies, and beyond. It reveals a diverse spectrum of citizen journalism scholarship in terms of theory, research, and practice, at both local and global levels. Specifically, our conclusions are as follows.

First, as indicated by topic modeling and semantic network analysis, the citizen journalism scholarship included in our analysis focuses on three major themes: (1) the impact of digital technology, (2) audience engagement and the identity of professional journalists, and (3) new modalities and civic engagement. The question of how new platforms and technologies enable citizen journalism has become crucial with the abundance of digital platforms. Additionally, traditional media have been exploring approaches to incorporate citizen-generated content for their use. It thus raises questions about journalistic standards and the identity of professional journalists. Furthermore, whether citizen engagement in the information space has an impact on other aspects of civic life remains another important area in prior citizen journalism scholarship.

Second, our bibliometric analysis indicates that collaborations have remained within small groups, although there have been many collaborations among scholars, organizations, and countries. Moreover, we find that Journalism Practice, Journalism, New Media & Society, Journalism Studies, and Digital Journalism are the top five most influential journals among the scholarship included in our analysis. Additionally, we identify four important literatures in our list (Goode, 2009;Nah et al., 2015;Thurman, 2008;Wall, 2015). The literature citation network results by thematic areas indicate that there is a significant amount of communication within the scholarly works among literature related to audience engagement and professional journalists' identity, and the new modalities and civic engagement themes.

Third, our results from keyword co-occurrence suggest that prior literature primarily examines citizen journalism from the perspective of digital technology and social media. In contrast, the impact of citizen journalism on community building and democracy is less studied. The year trend of keywords co-occurrence suggests that the theme of digital media impact is more prevalent in scholarship published in earlier periods. Keywords related to citizen journalism's role in civic engagement, journalistic ethics, and citizen journalism landscape in various countries are more common in recent periods.

Fourth, we identify major theories, methodologies, the unit of analyses, as well as the types and outcomes of citizen journalism through manual coding analysis. We find that citizen journalism scholars have adopted a wide range of theories, including gatekeeping theory, agenda-setting theory, public sphere theory, and communicative action theory. It has also adopted a wide range of methodological approaches, including content analysis, interview, survey, and field observation. Regarding the unit of analysis, it has focused more on individual journalists, both professionals and citizens.

Fifth, we examine the main type of citizen journalism by investigating the platform, medium, and relationship with professional journalists. With respect to the platform of citizen journalism, we observe a decrease in the number of studies examining mainstream and citizen-based news sites since 2017, while the share of social media platformsfocused studies has been increasing. Text is the primary medium among the articles in our analysis, though it is worth noting that image and video are also highly significant. The primary focus of the prior literature is how citizens produce news on their own or comment online. Less studied are on how citizens and journalists jointly create content and how individual professional journalists adopt user-created content.

Lastly, we find that online participation is the major participation setting of citizen journalism outcomes across the years. Protests, local public affairs and community building, crisis reporting, and civic participation are the major participation types among the articles that have addressed citizen journalism outcomes.

The study has the following caveats. First, our search is limited to English-only articles. As observed in our country/region co-authorship analysis, much of the scholarly works from Chile, China, Japan, Russia, and Spain included in our analysis have been published more recently. It is plausible that the citizen journalism literature in those countries has been published earlier in different languages. Second, this study is inherently exploratory in nature. We rely on WOS as our primary source and use EBSCO and JSTOR as complementary. The search keywords we use may not cover every combination of keywords that have been used in citizen journalism scholarship. Therefore, the document list included for analysis may not be comprehensive. Future work may construct a dictionary of keywords prevalent among major citizen journalism scholarship. Additionally, other literature databases can be examined more holistically to obtain a more comprehensive list of citizen journalism literature. Lastly, the current study focuses on the Web era where the digital revolution happened. It does not provide information on the evolution of pre-Web citizen reporting. Citizen journalism prior to 1994 was mostly accessible to those within certain industries or with specific technological skills (Nah, 2008). The contribution to citizen journalism in this period, although less widespread due to technological constraints, laid the groundwork for participatory media culture. Future work may expand the search years to explore the characteristics of citizen journalism in the pre-Web era.

To aid future research in citizen journalism, we conclude by proposing recommendations based on our results. First, future studies should consider collaborative networks at the local, national, and global levels. Given the network is centralized in the U.S., future scholarship should explore the establishment of networks in the global south for cross-country and cross-cultural comparisons. Moreover, scholarship should consider publishing its works in other communication and interdisciplinary journals as it intersects with other disciplines such as sociology, political science, information and data science, and computer science among others. While citizen journalism scholars have adopted a wide range of theories, they should continue to use diverse theoretical perspectives and work on the development and advancement of theories in the rapidly evolving media and technology landscape, as well as across diverse cultures and countries. The integration of multiple theoretical perspectives may enrich the explanation of citizen journalism scholarship (Nah and Yamamoto, 2019). Finally, while citizen journalism scholarship has adopted a wide range of methodological approaches, including surveys, case studies, experiments, and field research, it has focused more on individual journalists, both professional and citizen, as the primary unit of analysis. While exploring interactions between professional and citizen journalists can remain a central focus, citizen journalism scholarship could greatly benefit from a multi-level approach that encompasses not only individuals or microlevel but also organizations at the meso-level, and communities and countries at the macro-level or the system level. This can be achieved through cross-community, cross-country, and cross-cultural comparisons.

As previously discussed, against the backdrop of the burst of digital technologies and social media platforms, citizen journalism scholarship has faced a decline (as shown in Figure 1). This phenomenon is associated with the larger change in news consumption. Individuals are increasingly more often to get news from social media apps, which may have caused significant disruptions in traditional media (Pew Research Center, 2023a;2023b). It poses another question about civic outcomes: whether news consumption on social media is associated with lower levels of political knowledge and political participation compared to traditional news sources (Mitchell et al., 2020). Future studies may delve deeper into this area of inquiry.

In the meantime, this phenomenon also presents a growing number of opportunities with the newly emerging technologies, such as generative AI (ChatGPT, in particular). Therefore, it will be intriguing to examine how AI can further invigorate citizen journalism scholarship for community building and participatory democracy. However, it is important to note that there are potential drawbacks associated with employing AI techniques in journalistic production, such as fake news and misrepresentation in AIgenerated texts. In this vein, future scholarship should examine ethical and legal issues to inform policy implications in the context of participatory communication and news engagement, which are vital components for fostering healthier communities and democracies.

However, it is important to note that there are potential drawbacks associated with employing AI techniques in journalistic production, such as fake news and misrepresentation in AIgenerated texts. In this vein, future scholarship should examine ethical and legal issues to inform policy implications in the context of participatory communication and news engagement, which are vital components for fostering healthier communities and democracies.
sections.text_with_footnotes:
Our meta-analytical study maps out the citizen journalism scholarship spanning the last 30 years of journal articles published in the fields of journalism, communication, media, technology studies, and beyond. It reveals a diverse spectrum of citizen journalism scholarship in terms of theory, research, and practice, at both local and global levels. Specifically, our conclusions are as follows.

First, as indicated by topic modeling and semantic network analysis, the citizen journalism scholarship included in our analysis focuses on three major themes: (1) the impact of digital technology, (2) audience engagement and the identity of professional journalists, and (3) new modalities and civic engagement. The question of how new platforms and technologies enable citizen journalism has become crucial with the abundance of digital platforms. Additionally, traditional media have been exploring approaches to incorporate citizen-generated content for their use. It thus raises questions about journalistic standards and the identity of professional journalists. Furthermore, whether citizen engagement in the information space has an impact on other aspects of civic life remains another important area in prior citizen journalism scholarship.

Second, our bibliometric analysis indicates that collaborations have remained within small groups, although there have been many collaborations among scholars, organizations, and countries. Moreover, we find that Journalism Practice, Journalism, New Media & Society, Journalism Studies, and Digital Journalism are the top five most influential journals among the scholarship included in our analysis. Additionally, we identify four important literatures in our list (Goode, 2009;Nah et al., 2015;Thurman, 2008;Wall, 2015). The literature citation network results by thematic areas indicate that there is a significant amount of communication within the scholarly works among literature related to audience engagement and professional journalists' identity, and the new modalities and civic engagement themes.

Third, our results from keyword co-occurrence suggest that prior literature primarily examines citizen journalism from the perspective of digital technology and social media. In contrast, the impact of citizen journalism on community building and democracy is less studied. The year trend of keywords co-occurrence suggests that the theme of digital media impact is more prevalent in scholarship published in earlier periods. Keywords related to citizen journalism's role in civic engagement, journalistic ethics, and citizen journalism landscape in various countries are more common in recent periods.

Fourth, we identify major theories, methodologies, the unit of analyses, as well as the types and outcomes of citizen journalism through manual coding analysis. We find that citizen journalism scholars have adopted a wide range of theories, including gatekeeping theory, agenda-setting theory, public sphere theory, and communicative action theory. It has also adopted a wide range of methodological approaches, including content analysis, interview, survey, and field observation. Regarding the unit of analysis, it has focused more on individual journalists, both professionals and citizens.

Fifth, we examine the main type of citizen journalism by investigating the platform, medium, and relationship with professional journalists. With respect to the platform of citizen journalism, we observe a decrease in the number of studies examining mainstream and citizen-based news sites since 2017, while the share of social media platformsfocused studies has been increasing. Text is the primary medium among the articles in our analysis, though it is worth noting that image and video are also highly significant. The primary focus of the prior literature is how citizens produce news on their own or comment online. Less studied are on how citizens and journalists jointly create content and how individual professional journalists adopt user-created content.

Lastly, we find that online participation is the major participation setting of citizen journalism outcomes across the years. Protests, local public affairs and community building, crisis reporting, and civic participation are the major participation types among the articles that have addressed citizen journalism outcomes.

The study has the following caveats. First, our search is limited to English-only articles. As observed in our country/region co-authorship analysis, much of the scholarly works from Chile, China, Japan, Russia, and Spain included in our analysis have been published more recently. It is plausible that the citizen journalism literature in those countries has been published earlier in different languages. Second, this study is inherently exploratory in nature. We rely on WOS as our primary source and use EBSCO and JSTOR as complementary. The search keywords we use may not cover every combination of keywords that have been used in citizen journalism scholarship. Therefore, the document list included for analysis may not be comprehensive. Future work may construct a dictionary of keywords prevalent among major citizen journalism scholarship. Additionally, other literature databases can be examined more holistically to obtain a more comprehensive list of citizen journalism literature. Lastly, the current study focuses on the Web era where the digital revolution happened. It does not provide information on the evolution of pre-Web citizen reporting. Citizen journalism prior to 1994 was mostly accessible to those within certain industries or with specific technological skills (Nah, 2008). The contribution to citizen journalism in this period, although less widespread due to technological constraints, laid the groundwork for participatory media culture. Future work may expand the search years to explore the characteristics of citizen journalism in the pre-Web era.

To aid future research in citizen journalism, we conclude by proposing recommendations based on our results. First, future studies should consider collaborative networks at the local, national, and global levels. Given the network is centralized in the U.S., future scholarship should explore the establishment of networks in the global south for cross-country and cross-cultural comparisons. Moreover, scholarship should consider publishing its works in other communication and interdisciplinary journals as it intersects with other disciplines such as sociology, political science, information and data science, and computer science among others. While citizen journalism scholars have adopted a wide range of theories, they should continue to use diverse theoretical perspectives and work on the development and advancement of theories in the rapidly evolving media and technology landscape, as well as across diverse cultures and countries. The integration of multiple theoretical perspectives may enrich the explanation of citizen journalism scholarship (Nah and Yamamoto, 2019). Finally, while citizen journalism scholarship has adopted a wide range of methodological approaches, including surveys, case studies, experiments, and field research, it has focused more on individual journalists, both professional and citizen, as the primary unit of analysis. While exploring interactions between professional and citizen journalists can remain a central focus, citizen journalism scholarship could greatly benefit from a multi-level approach that encompasses not only individuals or microlevel but also organizations at the meso-level, and communities and countries at the macro-level or the system level. This can be achieved through cross-community, cross-country, and cross-cultural comparisons.

As previously discussed, against the backdrop of the burst of digital technologies and social media platforms, citizen journalism scholarship has faced a decline (as shown in Figure 1). This phenomenon is associated with the larger change in news consumption. Individuals are increasingly more often to get news from social media apps, which may have caused significant disruptions in traditional media (Pew Research Center, 2023a;2023b). It poses another question about civic outcomes: whether news consumption on social media is associated with lower levels of political knowledge and political participation compared to traditional news sources (Mitchell et al., 2020). Future studies may delve deeper into this area of inquiry.

In the meantime, this phenomenon also presents a growing number of opportunities with the newly emerging technologies, such as generative AI (ChatGPT, in particular). Therefore, it will be intriguing to examine how AI can further invigorate citizen journalism scholarship for community building and participatory democracy. However, it is important to note that there are potential drawbacks associated with employing AI techniques in journalistic production, such as fake news and misrepresentation in AIgenerated texts. In this vein, future scholarship should examine ethical and legal issues to inform policy implications in the context of participatory communication and news engagement, which are vital components for fostering healthier communities and democracies.

Department within the Scalable Analytics Institute (ScAi) under Professor Wei Wang's guidance, Dr. Kim's work focuses on leveraging multimodal inputs to develop practical AI solutions for pressing societal issues. Specializing in graph neural networks and multimodal learning, he pioneers innovative machine-learning frameworks spanning domains such as mental health, communication, and marketing. Dr. Kim's collaborative spirit is evident in his extensive collaborations with scholars from diverse fields, resulting in impactful contributions like machine learning models to detect depression symptoms and predict suicide risk levels. At USF, he continues to lead new research initiatives in partnership with experts from varied disciplines, showcasing his dedication to interdisciplinary exploration and the advancement of AI-driven solutions to real-world challenges.
